message: Built frontend-only Vue page editor (Tasks 1-11, subagent-driven). Stack settled: Vite+Vue3+TS, Pinia, Tailwind v4 (CSS-first), shadcn-vue on reka-ui, @lucide/vue (lucide-vue-next deprecated), hand-rolled drag/resize (no DnD lib). usePagesStore is the draft page-state contract. Publish/upload/drawing/device-API deliberately stubbed. 42 tests pass.

I1+M5 done: position-aware timeline reorder (drop tile onto another -> takes its slot index; sidebar page drops still append) + interaction tests (mounted drag updates store, reorder both directions, ToolRail localStorage wiring). 46 tests pass.

Prototype feedback round: draw-to-place element creation (tool click only activates; drag a rect on canvas to create), working pen (DrawingLayer capture -> DrawingEl/DrawingWidget SVG), size-scaled clock/calendar fonts (clock capped by width), Backspace/Delete + ToolRail trash deletes selected element. 55 tests pass; verified in real browser at scale 1.2.

Feedback round 3 (6 subagent-driven changes + review fixes, commits 21009bd..c66036b): unified global colour panel (colour on BaseEl, setElementColour); pen stroke scales on resize (natW/natH viewBox); live element creation with auto-switch-to-select and pointer-events gating so creation tools always create; drawings in thumbnails; smooth pen via perfect-freehand (filled ink paths, tap-to-dot). Backspace+Delete both verified deleting in-browser. 81 tests pass.
Target display pinned: Waveshare 7.3in E6 (Spectra 6), 800x480, 6-colour, full-refresh only. Persisted from round-one design doc into context/hardware.md + decisions.md.

Device render approach = C: ONE renderer written in Rust, compiled to WASM for the editor (draws to canvas) and native for the device (draws to framebuffer). Single renderer => pixel parity by construction, no two-codebase parity treadmill. Chosen partly as a deliberate learn-Rust / over-engineer-for-fun call. Replaces the editor's Vue HTML/CSS widgets with WASM-drawn pixels; Vue keeps the editing chrome.

SUPERSEDES the earlier 'approach C' decision. Render approach = A: TWO renderers. Editor keeps its Vue render as a rough DESIGN surface (allowed to be approximate). Device renderer is SEPARATE, written in Rust (the fun/learn-Rust goal lives here). No pixel-perfect editor<->device parity requirement — only 'no glaring differences'. The device serves its real 800x480 6-colour render over a web endpoint, which is the authoritative 'what it looks like' preview (and the hardware stand-in pre-delivery), so the editor needs no image-quantise preview of its own. Pixel-comparison tests become a generous-threshold guardrail against glaring divergence, not a tautology.

Refresh-driven product simplification (after watching real Spectra 6 refresh speed): (1) DROP the clock element entirely — nothing updates minute-to-minute. (2) Keep the DATE, as a calendar variant. (3) DROP the timeline/auto-loop — the panel is too slow to cycle pages. (4) Pages model = (b): keep MULTIPLE saved layouts (drafts), publish ONE as the live display; no auto-cycle. Consequence: the device render trigger collapses to 'render on publish + one scheduled re-render at midnight for the date rollover' — the clock-vs-refresh tension is gone. Ripples into the existing editor: remove Timeline/TimelineItem, ClockWidget/ClockOptions, clock tool in ToolRail, clock in thumbnails, timeline reorder; add a date-only calendar variant; DocState drops 'timeline', gains a 'live page' pointer.

Device storage model = (1): the device stores the WHOLE document (all draft pages + livePageId + uploaded images) and is the single source of truth. Editor GETs the full document to hydrate, POSTs the full document to publish, marks one page live. Drafts are shared across any browser that opens the editor. Rejected (2) live-page-only (drafts would be trapped per-browser, wrong for a shared board).

Editor hosting = (a): the device serves the built Vue editor + the API + the preview, all one origin on the LAN (http://corkboard.local/), no internet needed. Dev workflow: Vite dev server proxies API/preview to a running device-server instance. FUTURE (deferred, keep simple now): owner has ideas for decoupling the editor from the display/device — revisit later; do not build for it yet.

Routine stack calls (owner delegated): device web framework = axum; on-device storage = plain files (document.json + images/ dir, GC unreferenced images on publish); renderer crates to be specified in the design doc.

Calendar events source = B: external feed via Google Calendar's secret iCal (ICS) URL. The device fetches the ICS over HTTPS and parses it; events are resolved AT RENDER TIME on the device, NOT stored in the document. CalendarEl stops carrying a frozen events[] list and instead references a feed + filter (today/week/date variant). Refresh cadence: re-fetch + re-render at the midnight tick and on publish (no new refresh machinery; once-a-day freshness is fine for slow e-paper). Editor preview shows SAMPLE/placeholder events (rough design surface); device preview shows the real ones. NEW CATEGORY introduced: device config/secrets. The secret iCal URL is a credential -> lives in device-side config, kept OUT of the published document and OUT of git. Rejected C (full Google OAuth) as too heavy for a household board.

REFINES the render-trigger decision. Refresh model = decouple feed-polling from panel-refresh. (1) Poll the iCal feed on an interval, default 60min, settable in device config. (2) On every poll/publish/manual-trigger: render the live page to a buffer, HASH it, compare to the currently-displayed frame's hash, and push to the panel ONLY if it changed -- structurally prevents needless slow/flashy e-paper refreshes and panel wear. (3) NEW endpoint POST /api/refresh forces an immediate fetch+re-render now, wired to a 'Refresh now' button in the web UI. The hourly poll + change-detection subsumes the midnight date rollover (date flips within the hour); a dedicated midnight tick is optional/deferred. Config gains pollIntervalMinutes (default 60).

CORRECTS the prior refresh-model entry. Change-detection is SEMANTIC, not pixel-based: do NOT render-and-hash to compare. The device tracks the currently-displayed CALENDAR CONTENT (resolved events + date for the live page). On each poll it re-resolves from the feed and compares that content; if it changed -> re-render + push to panel; if unchanged -> do nothing. Publish and POST /api/refresh always re-render + push. (Earlier 'hash the rendered frame' framing is withdrawn.)

Device mDNS hostname is a config value (config.json 'hostname', default 'corkboard' -> http://<hostname>.local/), not hardcoded. config.json now holds: feeds[], pollIntervalMinutes (default 60), hostname (default corkboard).

Text tool + shared bundled fonts. New TextEl (type:'text': text, font, align + shared colour/geometry) and a 'text' tool. Text content edited ON-CANVAS in place (TextOptions panel holds font/align/colour). Fonts: bundled self-hosted open files (NOT Google Fonts CDN) -- device loads them for the Rust renderer AND serves them to the editor as @font-face from one origin, so both shape from the same bytes and it works offline. Shared bundled font MANIFEST (assets/fonts/manifest.json, served at /fonts/manifest.json) lists what's available; NOT config.json. Seed set: Atkinson Hyperlegible (default), Inter, Caveat.

Font manifest format: JSON { fonts: [ { id (stable key stored in TextEl.font), name (picker label), default (bool fallback), faces: [ { weight, style, file } ] } ] }. STATIC font files only, not variable fonts, so the browser and the Rust shaper apply the exact same face. Both consumers read the one file: editor -> @font-face rules (src /fonts/<file>); Rust -> load+register per family/weight/style. v1 may ship Regular-only per family.

Fonts: ship STATIC font files (one file per weight) rather than variable fonts, so neither the browser nor the Rust shaper interpolates the weight axis -> both draw identical outlines (parity). Static/variable is a property of the font FILES, not the manifest. Also recorded: font manifest stays SEPARATE from config.json -- manifest is build-scoped, secret-free, in git, and read by the editor too; config.json is deploy-scoped per-device runtime state with secrets, out of git, device-only. Different lifecycle + trust boundary.
